examples.title: Dynamic Array Allocation and Deallocation
examples.description: Program demonstrating dynamic memory allocation for arrays using malloc() and free()
examples.explanation: malloc() allocates contiguous memory blocks at runtime. sizeof(int) ensures proper sizing. The returned void pointer is cast to int*. Always check for NULL to handle allocation failures. free() releases memory back to system to prevent leaks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for both input and display operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the dynamically allocated array

examples.title: Polynomial Representation using Array
examples.description: Represent and evaluate a polynomial using array where index = exponent and value = coefficient
examples.explanation: The array index represents the exponent, and the value at that index represents the coefficient. For polynomial 3x² + 2x + 1, array is [1, 2, 3]. This representation allows efficient access and evaluation using Horner's method or direct computation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for evaluation using Horner's method

examples.title: Structure for Sparse Polynomial
examples.description: Using structures to represent sparse polynomials efficiently with only non-zero terms
examples.explanation: Instead of storing all coefficients (including zeros), we store only non-zero terms using a structure with coefficient and exponent fields. This is memory-efficient for sparse polynomials like 5x¹⁰⁰ + 3x² + 2 where array would waste space.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of non-zero terms

examples.title: Dynamic Array Implementation (Python List)
examples.description: Demonstrating dynamic array behavior in Python with resize capability
examples.explanation: When the array fills up, a new larger array (typically 2x) is allocated, existing elements are copied over, and the old array is discarded. This achieves amortized O(1) for append operations despite occasional O(n) resize costs.
examples.timeComplexity: Amortized O(1) for append, O(n) for worst case resize
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of elements stored
